Facts.text: St. Celestine I ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Treaty of San Germano between Emperor Frederick II and Pope Gregory IX
Sogen Mugaku, founder of Engakuji temple, arrives in Japan from China
Albert I of Habsburg is  proclaimed King of the Germans after the defeat and death of King Adolf of Nassau at the Battle of Göllheim
Danish King Waldemar IV destroys Visby, Gotland
Tribunal convicts Agnes Waterhouse of witchcraft and sentences her to be the first British woman executed for the crime (Chelmsford, England)
Jews of New Amsterdam petition for a Jewish cemetery
Netherlands and Brandenburg sign military treaty
English Parliament confirms Navigation Act
English Parliament accepts Staple Act
Battle of Killiecrankie: Jacobite Scottish Highlanders under Viscount Dundee defeat royalist forces under General Hugh Mackay
Bank of England is granted a 12-year charter by Act of Parliament
Russia and Turkey sign peace treaty
Battle at Hango (Hangut): Russians defeat Swedish fleet
The second important victory of the Russian Navy: the Battle of Grengam
US Congress establishes the Department of Foreign Affairs, now referred to as the State Department
Spain and France sign peace treaty
Battle of Talavera: British and Spanish army versus French army
US troops destroy Fort Apalachicola, a Seminole fort, to punish Indians for harboring runaway slaves
The ship the "Duke of York" arrives with the first colonists at Nepean Bay, Kangaroo Island, South Australia
US Mint opens in Charlotte, North Carolina
Chartist riots break out in Birmingham, England
Fire destroys US mint at Charlotte, North Carolina
Battle of Mathias Point, Virginia - Rebel forces repel a Federal landing
Battle of St. Augustine Springs, New Mexico Territory
Confederate troops occupy Fort Fillmore, New Mexico
Massive typhoon hits Canton and Whampoa in China, killing at least 40,000 people
Steamer "Golden Gate" burns and sinks off the west coast of Mexico
Battle of Deep Bottom, also known as Darbytown, Strawberry Plains, New Market Road
Welsh settlers arrive at Chubut in Argentina
Alexander P. Ashbourne patents a process for refining coconut oil
Battle of Maiwand, at which Dr Watson is wounded, breaks out
Philip Pratt unveils the first American electric tricycle
Titus van Wyck succeeds M. de Savornin Lohman as governor of Suriname
14.75 inches (37.5 cm) of rainfall, Jewell, Maryland (state 24-hour record)
Dutch government of Pierson and Goeman Borgesius resigns
British ship SS Waratah is last seen en route from Durban to Cape Town; 211 on board are missing, and no trace of the ship is ever found
Felix Manalo registers the Iglesia ni Cristo with the Filipino government
Roda JC soccer team forms in Kerkrade
Socony 200, the first concrete barge in the US, is launched to carry oil into Flushing Bay, NY
Chicago race riot kills 15 white people and 23 Black people and injures 500
American defender Resolute beats Thomas Lipton's British challenger Shamrock IV 3-2 in the 14th America's Cup
Radio compass is used for the first time for aircraft navigation
Second government of Ruijs de Beerenbrouck forms in the Netherlands
International Geographical Union forms in Brussels, Belgium
VIII Summer Olympic Games close at the Stade Olympique Yves-du-Manoir in Paris, France
AVRO, Algemene Vereniging Radio Omroep (General Association of Radio Broadcasting), forms in the Netherlands
Construction of the Dike of Wieringermeerpolder, Netherlands, is finished
Chilean president Carlos Ibáñez is forced out
Grasshoppers in Iowa, Nebraska, and South Dakota destroy thousands of acres of crops
Paul Gorgoulov, assassin of French president Doumer, sentenced to death
French Socialist/Communist Party of the Popular Front forms
Floods at Yangtze Jiang and Huang He kill 200,000
Billboard magazine begins publishing bestseller charts
Bugs Bunny, Warner Bros. cartoon character created by Tex Avery and Bob Givens for the Looney Tunes and Merrie Melodies series, debuts in "A Wild Hare"
103°F is recorded in Cleveland, the highest temperature recorded in Cleveland in July
German army enters Ukraine
Japanese forces land in Indochina
Transport #11 departs Drancy, France, with 1000 French Jews sent to Auschwitz concentration camp; 13 survive until the war's end
772 British bombers attack Hamburg
First British jet fighter used in combat (Gloster Meteor)
Soviet Army frees Majdanek concentration camp
US troops occupy Le Mesnil-Herman/Hill 183 in Normandy
Cubs purchase pitcher Hank Borowy from NY Yankees
US Communist Party forms
Boston Red Sox's Rudy York hits two grand slams in one game and gets 10 RBIs
Australia set 404 to win against England at Headingley
Otto Skorzeny escapes an internment camp near Darmstadt, germany
De Havilland Comet, the world’s first commercial jet airliner, makes its maiden flight
US President Harry Truman promises aid to Taiwan
Swedish race walker John Mikaelsson wins back-to-back gold medals in the 10,ooo m event at the Helsinki Olympics, having won the corresponding race in London in 1948
Dizzy Dean,Al Simmons, "Chief" Bender, Bobby Wallace, Harry Wright, Ed Barrow, Bill Klem, and Tom Connolly are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
Vatican disallows priests from working in factories during holidays
Armistice divides Vietnam into two countries
Austria regains full independence after four-power occupation since WWII
Israeli passenger plane shot down above Bulgaria, killing 58
St. James' Theatre in London closes
Abbas Ali Baig scores 112 for India against England on debut

Baseball's new Continental League is formally announced by attorney William Shea; teams in Denver, Colorado; Houston, Texas; Minneapolis–St. Paul, Minnesota; New York City; and Toronto, Ontario are confirmed, with three more to be named later
William Shea announces plans to have a baseball team in New York City in 1961
Mariner 2 launches to Venus for a flyby mission
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
Fritz Von Erich defeats Verne Gagne in Omaha to become NWA champion
General Amin al-Hafez becomes President of Syria
"Sometimes a Great Notion," the second novel by Ken Kesey, is published by Viking Press
Pierre Harmel forms Belgian government
Helmond Sport football team founded in Helmond, Netherlands
PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonsets up a commission to study the causes of urban violence
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Race Riot in Gary Indiana
Expos beat White Sox 10-6 in the annual Hall of Fame game
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
The F-15 Eagle flies for the first time
40th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Miami 14, All-Stars 3 (54,103 attendees)
Walter Blum becomes the sixth jockey to ride 4,000 winners
House Judiciary Committee votes 27-11 to recommend Nixon's impeachment
The British government closes its consulate in Angola following increased fighting between the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola and South African troops
Japanese ex-premier Tanaka arrested (Lockheed Affair)
Cleveland Indians' Duane Kuiper becomes the third player in MLB history to hit two bases-loaded triples in a game at Yankee Stadium
France performs a nuclear test
Palestinian throws a hand grenade at Jewish children in Antwerp, Belgium, killing one
104°F (40.3°C) in Garmersdorf (German record)
Iraqi fighter jets attack the central Iranian city of Arak, prompting Iran to threaten a missile attack on Gulf states supporting Iraq
First expedited salvage of Titanic wreck begins by RMS Titanic, Inc.
John Demjanjuk, accused Nazi "Ivan the Terrible," testifies in Israel
Salt Lake City Trappers lose 7-5 to Billings Mustangs, ending their professional record-winning streak at 29 consecutive games
Boston's worst traffic jam in 30 years
General Sein Lwin succeeds San Yu as the 6th President of Burma
RadioShack announces Tandy 1000 SL computer
Belarus declares independence
TV Guide publishes its 2000th edition
Astros begins 26-game road trip to make room for Republican National Convention
Dimitri Lepikov, Vladimir Pychenko, Veniamin Taianovitch, and Evgueni Sadovyi swim a world record in the 4 x 200 m freestyle (7:11.95)
Japanese swimmer Kyoko Iwasaki wins the 200 m breaststroke in Barcelona to become the youngest to win an Olympic gold medal at 14 years and 6 days old
Detroit infielder Travis Fryman hits for the cycle, but the Tigers lose 12-7 to the NY Yankees
Javier Sotomayor sets a high jump world record at 2.45 m
Mafia bombs historical buildings in Rome, Milan, and Vatican City, killing 5
NBC TV is awarded coverage of the 1996 Olympics for $456 million
New York MetAnthony Youngwins, ending his losing streak at 27 games
Korean War Veterans Memorial is dedicated in Washington, D.C.
Bomb explodes at Atlanta Olympic Park, killing one and injuring 110
Brazilian pair Jackie Silva and Sandra Pires win inaugural women's beach volleyball gold medal at the Atlanta Olympics
David Sales makes 210 on first class cricket debut for Northants vs. Worcs
Detroit Tigers retire pitching great Hal Newhouser's #16
Si Zerrouk massacre in Algeria kills about 50 people
A Sukhoi Su-27 fighter crashes during an airshow at Lviv, Ukraine, killing 85 and injuring more than 100 in the largest airshow disaster in history
NASA postpones all Shuttle flights pending an investigation into the foam-shedding problem on Discovery's external tanks
The Federal Republic of Germany is deemed guilty in the loss of Bashkirian 2937 and DHL Flight 611 because it is illegal to outsource flight surveillance

Two news helicopters from Phoenix, Arizona television stations KNXV and KTVK collide over Steele Indian School Park in central Phoenix while covering a police chase; there are no survivors. It is the worst civil aviation incident in Phoenix history.
1,000 inmates escape from a prison in Benghazi, Libya
100 people are killed and 1,500 injured in a crackdown against protesters in Cairo, Egypt

First Przewalski’s horse, whose wild populations were believed extinct in 1969, is born via artificial insemination at the Smithsonian Conservation Biology Institute as part of a worldwide effort to rebuild the wild herds
Liberia shuts down most of its borders due to fears about the spread of Ebola epidemic
Fiat Chrysler fined record $105 million by US regulators over their number of car recalls
The Shanghai Composite Index drops 8.5% in one day
Reince Priebus resigns as Chief of Staff to US PresidentDonald Trumpafter just over 6 months, making it the shortest non-interim tenure
CBS Chairman Leslie Moonves is accused of sexual misconduct in The New Yorker by journalist Ronan Farrow
American swimmer Caeleb Dressel wins three gold medals in one day at the World Championships in Gwangju, South Korea: 50 m freestyle, 100 m butterfly, and mixed 4 × 100 m freestyle relay (WR 3:19.40)
At least 65 mourners are killed in a gun attack at a funeral by suspected Boko Haram militants near Maiduguri in northeast Nigeria
MLB Phillies revive the reviled 1979 all-burgundy alternate uniforms as part of a "retro night" promotion in a 15-7 loss to the Atlanta Braves at Citizens Bank Park, Philadelphia
USPresident Trumpcalls Baltimore a "disgusting, rat and rodent-infested mess" and holds Congress Rep Elijah Cummings responsible
Google decides its employees can work from home until July 2021, becoming the largest tech company to commit to working from home
China tests a missile with a hypersonic weapon system, later called “very close” to a Sputnik moment by a US general
Fiji retains the Olympic Rugby Sevens title with a 27-12 win over New Zealand in Tokyo, Japan, achieving only the second gold in the country's Olympic history
Florida records its highest one-day total of COVID-19 cases since the start of the pandemic at 21,683
Ítalo Ferreira of Brazil and American Carissa Moore claim inaugural men's and women's surfing gold medals at the Tokyo Olympic Games
Largest-ever repatriation of 17,000 looted Iraqi antiquities returns to Baghdad, including items from Hobby Lobby's Museum of the Bible and Cornell University
Swiss team of Jolanda Neff, Sina Frei, and Linda Indergand make a clean sweep of the medals in the women's cross-country mountain bike event at the Tokyo Olympics
Triathlete Flora Duffy wins first Olympic gold medal for Bermuda in the women's event at the Tokyo Games
170-carat pink diamond "Lulo Rose," thought to be the largest discovered in 300 years, is announced as found in Angola
City of Phoenix, Arizona, heat record continues with 28 consecutive days of temperatures at or above 110°F (43.3°C); overnight low falls below 90°F (32.2°C) for the first time in 18 days
Rocket attack on Israeli-controlled Golan Heights kills 12 children and threatens all-out war with Hezbollah based in Lebanon
First commercial flight carrying over 400 passengers flies from Moscow, Russia, to Pyongyang, North Korea
Passenger train carrying about 100 people derails in southern Germany, injuring at least 40 people and killing three

Battle of Bouvines: King Philip II of France vs. Holy Roman Emperor Otto IV andKing Johnof England; as a result, John loses Normandy and his other possessions in France, hence his nickname John "Lackland"
Nicolaus Copernicusformally installed as an Augustinian canon of Frauenberg Cathedral at Frombork
First Christian missionary in Japan, Jesuit priestFrancis Xavier, reaches Japan but is not permitted to enter any port until August 15
Walter Raleighbrings the first tobacco to England from Virginia
Prince of OrangeFrederick Henrycaptures the castle of Gennep after a siege
Oliver Cromwelldefeats Royalists at Battle of Gainsborough
BritishQueen Annedismisses premier Robert Haley
Maximilien Robespierreis overthrown in a coup in Paris
Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla, a Spanish Catholic priest and leader of Mexican independence, is defrocked and excommunicated by the church
July Revolution breaks out in Paris, opposing the laws of KingCharles X
Union GeneralGeorge McClellantakes command from McDowell of the Army of the Potomac
Dutch painterVincent van Goghshoots himself in Auvers-sur-Oise and dies of his injuries 2 days later
German KaiserWilhelm IIdelivers his "Hun" speech at Bremerhaven, addressing German troops departing to suppress the Boxer Rebellion in China, later used as propaganda in World War I
Orville Wrightsuccessfully tests the Wright Military Flyer, the world's first military airplane, with a record flight of 1 hour, 12 minutes, and 40 seconds, flying approximately 64 km (40 mi)
Frederick Bantingand Charles Best isolate insulin at the University of Toronto
Mel Ott, 18, hits his first league home run (inside the park)
Billboard publishes its first singles record chart; "I'll Never Smile Again" byTommy Dorsey, with vocals byFrank Sinatra, is ranked #1
New York Yankees catcherYogi Berrastarts a record 148-game errorless streak
US Vice-presidentRichard Nixonnominated for presidential candidate at Republican convention in Chicago
Martin Luther King Jr.is jailed in Albany, Georgia
PresidentLyndon B. Johnsonsigns a bill requiring cigarette manufacturers to print health warnings about the effects of smoking on all cigarette packages
Lou Boudreau, Earle Combs,Ford Frick, and Jesse Haines are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York
Former BeatleJohn Lennonis granted a green card for permanent residence in the US
Portuguese President António Ramalho Eanes fires PremierMário Soares

Modern/Atco Records releases "Bella Donna," the debut solo album by singer-songwriterStevie NicksofFleetwood Mac; hit singles include "Stop Draggin' My Heart Around" withTom Petty, "Edge of Seventeen," and "Leather and Lace" with Don Henley
Alan Menkenand Howard Ashman's musical "Little Shop of Horrors" opens Off-Broadway at the Orpheum Theatre in NYC
Indian Prime MinisterIndira Gandhi's first visit to the US in almost 11 years
Gaylord PerryjoinsNolan RyanandSteve Carltonto reach 3,500 career strikeouts, also winning his first game as a Kansas City Royal
Montreal Expos first basemanPete Rosecollects his record 3,503rd career single, passingTy Cobbwith a hit off former Phillies teammate Steve Carlton at Veterans Stadium in Philadelphia
Ingrid Kristiansenof Norway becomes the first woman to run 10,000 m in under 31 minutes, setting the world record at 30:59.42 in Oslo, Norway
Baseball starTommy Johncommits a record three errors on one play as the Yankees rout the Brewers 16-3
Atlanta BraveDale Murphybecomes the 10th player to achieve six RBIs in an inning (6th)
Zsa Zsa Gaborbegins a 3-day jail sentence for slapping a police officer in Beverly Hills
Nelson Mandelaannounces a general strike to protest the removal of South African PresidentF. W. de Klerkfrom power and to demand free elections

A group of 321 Filipino armed soldiers called "Magdalo" takes over Oakwood Premier Ayala Center in Makati City to show the Filipino people the alleged corruption of theGloria Macapagal-Arroyoadministration
Queen Elizabeth IIopens the 30th Olympic Games in London with some help from 007
Obamareaffirms Israel's "right to defend itself" but condemns civilian casualties in Gaza
Amazon founderJeff Bezosbriefly becomes world's richest person at $91.4B, overtakingBill Gatesfor half a day
Boy Scouts of American Chief Scout Executive Michael Surbaugh issues an apology for politically motivated remarks made by PresidentDonald Trumpat rally attended by 30,000 scouts
American singer-songwriterNeil Diamondgives a small thank-you concert to firefighters near his home in Colorado
US congressmanJohn Lewisbecomes the first black lawmaker to lie in state in the Rotunda in Washington D.C.
WHO headTedros Adhanom Ghebreyesusstates that COVID-19 is "easily the most severe" global health emergency the WHO has faced
American gymnast and four-time Olympic gold medallistSimone Bileswithdraws from the women's team final at the Tokyo Games citing need to focus on her mental health; also misses individual finals
Australian swimmer Ariarne Titmus retains her 400 m freestyle crown at the Paris Olympics in 3:57.49, defeating Canada's Summer McIntosh andKatie Ledeckyof the US
Australian swimmerEmma McKeonwins her career sixth gold and 14th Olympic medal over three Games as part of the victorious 4 x 100 m freestyle relay team in Paris
Baseball Hall of Fame induction ceremony is held for Dick Allen, Dave Parker, CC Sabathia,Ichiro Suzuki, and Billy Wagner in Cooperstown, New York
First example of quarantine in Ragusa (now Dubrovnik); city council passes a law stating newcomers from plague areas must isolate for 30 days (later 40 days, quaranta in Italian)
Firsttransatlantic telegraph cablecomes ashore at Heart's Content, Newfoundland, after being laid out 1,686 miles by Isambard Kingdom Brunel's Great Eastern steamship
World War I: Allied troops reach the Yser Canal in the prelude to the Battle of Passchendaele
North Korea and the United Nations sign armistice to stop fighting and divide Korea at the 38th parallel
Kūkai, Japanese Buddhist monk (founded Shingon school of Buddhism), born at Zentsū-ji temple, Shikoku (d. 835)
Francesco Corteccia, Italian composer, born in Florence, Florentine Republic (d. 1571)
Elizabeth Talbot, Countess of Shrewsbury and builder of country houses, born in Derbyshire, England (d. 1608)
Heinrich Pape, German composer and organist, born in Ratzeburg, Prince-Bishopric of Ratzeburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1663)
Murad IV, Sultan of Turkey (1623-40) who conquered Baghdad, born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1640)
Edward Montagu, 1st Earl of Sandwich, English soldier, admiral and patron to Samuel Pepys, born in Barnwell (d. 1672)
Jean Bernoulli, Swiss mathematician, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1748)
Jeremiah Dixon, English surveyor and astronomer, born in Cockfield, County Durham, England (d. 1779)
Jakob Zupan, Slovenian composer, born in Prevoje pri Šentvidu, Venetian Slovenia (d. 1810)
Jeanne Baré [Baret], French explorer (first woman to circumnavigate the world with Bougainville expedition, initially disguised as a man), born in France (d. 1807)
François-Hippolyte Barthélémon, French composer, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1808)
Samuel Smith, American politician, Senator and Representative from Maryland, born in Carlisle, Pennsylvania (d. 1839)
Charlotte Corday, French revolutionary who murdered Jean-Paul Marat in a bath, born in Saint-Saturnin, France (d. 1793)
Jakob Aall, Norwegian journalist and statesman, born in Porsgrunn, Norway (d. 1844)
Thomas Campbell, Scottish poet (Hohenlinden, Battle of the Baltic), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 1844)
Mauro Giuliani, Italian musician and composer, born in Bisceglie, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1829)
Denis Davydov, Russian general and poet, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1839)
George Onslow, British-French composer, chiefly of chamber music, born in Clermont-Ferrand, France (d. 1853)
George Biddell Airy, English mathematician and 7th Astronomer Royal, born in Alnwick, Northumberland, England (d. 1892)
Benedikt Randhartinger, Austrian composer, born in Ruprechtshofen, Austria (d. 1893)
Thomas Lanier Clingman, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Huntsville, North Carolina (d. 1897)
John Franklin Farnsworth, American politician and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Eaton, Canada (d. 1897)
Thomas George Bonney, English geologist, born in Rugeley, United Kingdom (d. 1923)
Giosuè Carducci, Italian writer and Nobel Prize Laureate 1906 regarded as the official national poet of modern Italy, born in Pietrasanta, Italy (d. 1907)
Ranald Slidell Mackenzie, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Westchester County, New York (d. 1889)
Friedrich Ernst Dorn, German physicist who discovered that radon is emitted from radium, born in Guttstadt, Province of Prussia (now Warmia in Poland) (d. 1916)
Loránd Eötvös, Hungarian physicist (introduced idea of molecular surface tension), born in Buda, Hungary (d. 1919)
Vladimir de Pachmann, Russian-German pianist, born in Odessa, Ukraine (d. 1933)
John Hopkinson, British physicist and electrical engineer (Hopkinson's Law), born in Manchester (d. 1898)
Edward Onslow Ford, English sculptor, born in Islington, London, England  (d. 1901)
Vladimir Korolenko, Russian writer (The Blind Musician), born in Zhitomir, Volhynian Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1921)
E. A. Wallis Budge, English orientalist and museum curator (British Museum), born in Bodmin, Cornwall, England (d. 1934)
José Celso Barbosa, Puerto Rican physician, sociologist and political leader, born in Bayamón, Puerto Rico (d. 1921)
Enrique Granados, Spanish concert pianist, and composer (Goyescas), born in Lérida, Spain (d. 1916)
Henry Lincoln "Linc" Johnson, American attorney and politician, remembered as one of the most prominent early 20th century African-American Republicans, born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 1925)
Hilaire Belloc, Anglo-French author (Path to Rome), born in La Celle-Saint-Cloud, Seine-et-Oise, France (d. 1953)
Stanislav Binički, Serbian composer, born in Jasika, Kruševac, Principality of Serbia (d. 1942)
Ernst von Dohnányi [Dohnányi Ernő], Hungarian composer and conductor, born in Pozsony, Kingdom of Hungary, Austria-Hungary (d. 1960)
Francesco Gaeta, Italian poet (Il Libro Della Giovinezza), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1927)
Jack Laviolette, Canadian ice hockey player, born in Belleville, Ontario, Canada (d. 1960)
Jack Doscher, American baseball pitcher (1st son of a major leaguer to play MLB), born in Troy, New York (d. 1971)
Joe Tinker, American Baseball HOF shortstop (World Series 1907, 08 Chicago Orphans/Cubs) and manager (Cincinnati Reds, Chicago Cubs), born in Muscotah, Kansas (d. 1948)
Donald Crisp, British actor and director (How Green Was My Valley, Pollyana), born in Bow, London, England (d. 1974)
Geoffrey de Havilland, British aircraft designer, born in High Wycombe, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1965)
Ernst May, German architect and city planner (New Frankfurt), born in Frankfurt am Main (d. 1970)
Judith Lowry, American actress (The Effect of Gamma Rays on Man-in-the-Moon Marigolds), born in Fort Sill, Oklahoma (d. 1976)
Gerald F. Bogan, U.S. Navy aviator and vice admiral who served in World War I & II, born in Mackinac Island, Michigan (d. 1973)
Henri Longchambon, French politician, born in Clermont-Ferrand, Puy-de-Dôme, France (d. 1969)
Biz Mackey, American Baseball HOF catcher (5 × East-West All-Star Game; NgL World Series 1925 Hilldale Daisies, 1946 Newark Eagles), born in Eagle Pass, Texas (d. 1965)
Lena Amsel, Russian actress (Love Tragedy), born in Lódz, Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1929)
Queen Mother Moore, African-American civil rights activist and humanitarian, born in New Iberia, Louisiana (d. 1997)
Perc Hornibrook, Australian cricket slow-medium bowler (6 Tests; 17 wickets; best 7-92 1930), born in Obi Obi, Australia (d. 1976)
Charles Vidor, Hungarian director (Rhapsody, Gilda, Cover Girl), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1959)
Hans Haug, Swiss composer, born in Luasanne, Switzerland (d. 1967)
Clive Douglas, Australian composer (Kaditcha; Carwoola), conductor (Australian Broadcasting Commission, 1938-68, and educator, born in Rushworth, Australia (d. 1977)
Michail Stasinopoulos, Greek lawyer, educator, writer, and politician (President of Greece, 1974-75), born in Kalamata, Kingdom of Greece (d. 2002)
Nikolai Cherkasov, Russian actor (Ivan the Terrible), born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1966)
Anton Dolin [Sydney Francis Patrick Chippendall Healey-kay], British dancer (Girl From Petrovka), born in Slinfold, Sussex, England (d. 1983)
Kenneth Bainbridge, American physicist (measurements of mass differences between nuclear isotopes), born in Cooperstown, New York (d. 1996)
Robert L. May, American advertising copy writer and creator of Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer, born in Arverne, New York (d. 1976)
Herbert Jasper, Canadian physiologist and neurologist, born in La Grande, Oregon (d. 1999)
Jerzy Giedroyc, Polish writer and political activist, born in Minsk, Russian Empire (d. 2000)
Denis Rickett, British civil servant and private secretary to Clement Attlee, born in Hawthorns, Overton Road, Sutton, Surrey (d. 1997)
Mollie Phillips, British figure skater and judge (first woman to referee a world championship), born in London, England (d. 1994)
Ross Alexander, American stage and film actor (Boulder Dam, Captain Blood), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1937)
Hugh Jenkins, Baron Jenkins of Putney, British MP of Arts (Labour), born in Enfield, Middlesex (d. 2004)
Marcia Anastasia Christoforides, British philanthropist, art collector, and racehorse owner, born in Sutton, Surrey, England (d. 1994)
Julien Gracq [Louis Poirier], French writer (Andre Breton), born in Saint-Florent-le-Vieil, Maine-et-Loire, France (d. 2007)
Lupita Tovar, Mexican-born actress (Dracula), born in Matías Romero, Mexico (d. 2016)
Rayner Heppenstall, English novelist (The Blaze of Noon), born in Lockwood, Huddersfield, Yorkshire (d. 1981)
Wilfrid Oulton, British Royal Air Force officer, born in Monks Coppenhall, Cheshire (d. 1997)
Hilde Domin [Hilde Palm], German writer and poet, born in Cologne (d. 2006)
Igor Markevitch, Russian conductor (Le Paradis Perdu), born in Kyiv, Ukraine (d. 1983)
Vernon Elliott, British bassoonist and composer, born in London (d. 1996)
Eva Jones, German poet and novelist (Taboo), born in Berlin (d. 1996)
George L. Street III, American Navy Submariner and Medal of Honor recipient, born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 2000)
Mary Green, headmistress (Kidbrooke School), born in Wellingborough, Northamptonshire (d. 2004)
Vittorio Sereni, Italian poet (Diario d'Algeria), born in Luino, Italy (d. 1983)
Jack Iverson, Australian cricket spin bowler (5 Tests, 21 wickets, BB 6/27; Victoria CA), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1973)
Josef "Pips" Priller, German WWII fighter ace, born in Ingolstadt, German Empire (d. 1961)
Mario del Monaco, Italian opera singer (Verdi/Puccini), born in Florence, Italy (d. 1982)
(Elbert) "Skippy" Williams, American jazz tenor saxophonist and musical arranger, born in Tuscaloosa, Alabama (d. 1994)
Amha Selassie, Crown prince of Ethiopia and son of emperor Haile Selassi, born in Harar, Ethiopian Empire (d. 1997)
Keenan Wynn, American actor (Dr Strangelove, Absent Minded Professor), born in New York City (d. 1986)
Siegfried Reda, German church musician and composer, born in Bochum, Germany (d. 1968)
Bourvil [André Robert Raimbourg], French actor and singer (Le Corniaud; La Grande Vadrouille), born in Prétot-Vicquemare, France (d. 1970)
Isabelle Kelley, American social worker credited with instigating the US Federal Food Stamp Program, born in Ellington, Connecticut (d. 1997)
John Cunningham, British Royal Air Force night fighter ace (WW II), born in Croydon, Surrey, England (d. 2002)
Moses Rascoe, American blues singer, born in Windsor, North Carolina (d. 1994)
Eero Sipilä, Finnish composer, born in Hailuoto, Finland (d. 1972)
Leonard Rose, American concert cellist (New York Philharmonic, 1943-51), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1984)
Per Hjort Albertsen, Norwegian organist, composer, and educator, born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 2015)
(Henry) "Homer" Haynes, American country music comedian and guitarist (Homer & Jethro - "The Battle of Kookamonga"), born near Knoxville, Tennessee (d. 1971)
James Munn, Scottish educationalist and commissioner (1977 Munn Report on education), born in Stirling, Scotland (d. 2009)
Émile Genest, French Canadian actor (The Man from U.N.C.L.E., Les Plouffe), born in Quebec City, Canada (d. 2003)
Adolfo Celi, Italian film actor and director (Next Man, Murders in Rue Morgue), born in Messina, Sicily (d. 1986)
Bob Thiele, American record producer (Impulse! Records), born in Sheepshead Bay, Brooklyn, New York (d. 1996)
Lillian Hayman, American actress (Leslie Uggams Show), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1994)

Norman Lear, American Peabody and Emmy Award-winning TV writer and producer (All in The Family; One Day at a Time; The Jeffersons; Maude), and film director (Cold Turkey), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 2023)[1][2]
Masutatsu Oyama, Founder of Japanese Kyokushin Karate, born in Gimje, Jeollabuk-do, Japanese Korea (d. 1994)
Otar Vasilisdze Taktakishvili, Georgian composer, conductor, teacher, and musicologist, born in Tbilisi, Georgia SSR (d. 1989)
Vincent Canby, American movie critic (NY Times), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2000)
